title: CP 09: Finding the Ka value for a weak acid

cards.side1: How must a pH meter be prepared before measuring unknown solutions?
cards.side2: It must be calibrated using standard buffer solutions of known pH (usually pH 4, 7, and 10), washing the probe with distilled water between each buffer.

cards.side1: How is the half-equivalence point found on a pH titration curve, and why is it useful?
cards.side2: Find the volume of base required for neutralization (equivalence point) and halve it. The pH at this half-equivalence volume is equal to the pKa of the weak acid.

cards.side1: How do you calculate the acid dissociation constant (Ka) of a weak acid from its pKa?
What is another way to find Ka without plotting a full titration curve?
cards.side2: Measure the pH of the weak acid. Use [H+] = 10^-pH. Assuming [H+] ≈ [A-], calculate Ka = [H+]² / [HA], where [HA] is the initial concentration of the acid.

cards.side1: What are the common safety precautions in this acid-base experiment?
cards.side2: Wear safety goggles, as acids and alkalis can be irritants or corrosive. Clean up any drips from the burette.
